raw_case:
Dedifferentiated parosteal osteosarcoma with a novel low-grade leiomyosarcomatous and high-grade conventional osteosarcomatous differentiation: Case report and review of literature


Introduction & importance

Parosteal osteosarcoma is an uncommon, low-grade, well differentiated malignant bone neoplasm with a more favorable prognosis compared to other types of osteosarcoma. Dedifferentiation is a well-known phenomenon, observed in 16–24.6 % of cases, leads to a combination of low-grade fibroblastic osteosarcoma and high-grade sarcoma. The dedifferentiated component is typically high-grade conventional osteosarcoma, although unusual variants such as spindle cell sarcoma, telangiectatic osteosarcoma, and rhabdomyosarcomatous dedifferentiation may arise. This case report contributes significant information to the sparse literature on dedifferentiated parosteal osteosarcoma due to its distinctive characteristics.

Case presentation

We describe a rare presentation of dedifferentiated parosteal osteosarcoma in a 44-year-old male with a lesion in the left femur. The dedifferentiated component included low-grade leiomyosarcoma alongside high-grade conventional osteosarcoma, presenting a diagnostic challenge, particularly with limited biopsy material. Diagnosis was confirmed using fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) for MDM2 gene amplification, emphasizing the importance of molecular diagnostic testing in such complex cases.

Clinical discussion

This case highlights a rare presentation of dedifferentiated parosteal osteosarcoma (DPO) with low-grade leiomyosarcomatous and high-grade osteosarcomatous components, posing significant diagnostic challenges. Accurate diagnosis required a multidisciplinary approach, including imaging, histopathology, and MDM2 gene amplification testing, especially given the limitations of initial biopsy samples. Early recognition of dedifferentiation is critical due to its impact on metastasis risk and treatment planning. The case underscores the need for personalized treatment strategies and vigilant long-term follow-up in managing rare bone tumors like DPO.

Conclusion

This case presenting a distinctive pattern of dedifferentiation is, to our knowledge, the first documented case of low-grade leiomyosarcoma occurring in parosteal osteosarcoma. As it enhances the clinical understanding by broadening the range of dedifferentiated components identified in parosteal osteosarcoma and highlights the diagnostic difficulties associated with these uncommon bone tumors.

Highlights

•Parosteal osteosarcoma (PO) often has a favorable prognosis but may dedifferentiate, significantly increasing its clinical complexity.•Presented a rare dedifferentiated PO case in a 44-year-old male with low-grade leiomyosarcoma, emphasizing unique diagnostic challenges.•Emphasized the importance of recognizing dedifferentiation in PO for appropriate management.1

Introduction

Parosteal osteosarcoma (PO) is a low-grade, well-differentiated, malignant bone-forming neoplasm that originates from the cortical bone surface. Although rare, it constitutes only 4 % of all osteosarcomas and represents the most common subtype of surface osteosarcomas [1]. It typically occurs in skeletally mature patients aged 20–30 years, with a predilection for females [2]. Preferential locations of PO are the metaphysis of the posterior aspect of the distal femur in approximately 70 % of cases, less commonly in the proximal tibia, humerus, and rarely in the ulna [3], skull [4], mandible [5], rib, clavicle, and tarsal bone [6].A significant diagnostic challenge with PO is dedifferentiation, observed in approximately 16–24.6 % of cases [[7], [8], [9]] and this phenomenon was first recognized by many reports and studies in the 50s to70s of the last century [[10], [11], [12], [13], [14], [15]]. De-differentiation can occur synchronously, in which low-grade fibroblastic osteosarcoma co-exists with high-grade sarcoma de novo in the first presentation or metachronous in recurrence, especially in the setting of incomplete surgical excision [13,16,17]. Dedifferentiated parosteal osteosarcoma (DPO) entails the progression from a low-grade, well-differentiated neoplasm to a high-grade, aggressive variant, markedly elevating the risk of metastasis and deteriorating the overall prognosis. High-grade conventional osteosarcoma is the most prevalent type of dedifferentiation; however, less common variants such as undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma, telangiectatic osteosarcoma, and rhabdomyosarcoma have also been documented [[17], [18], [19]]. This case is unique for its rare combination of low-grade leiomyosarcoma and high-grade conventional osteosarcoma, an occurrence that, to our knowledge, has not been reported before.Diagnosing dedifferentiation is particularly difficult due to the absence of definitive clinical or radiological indicators that differentiate DPO from its usual variant. Imaging modalities such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and computed tomography (CT) may disclose characteristics indicative of dedifferentiation, including medullary involvement or regions of radiolucency; however, these findings are not limited to dedifferentiated patients [9]. Diagnosis becomes increasingly challenging when biopsy samples are limited or fail to encompass the high-grade component. A complete diagnostic approach is often required, incorporating histology, immunohistochemical testing, and cytogenetic molecular techniques, such as MDM2 gene amplification testing via Fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH), to confirm the diagnosis [25].This case report expands the understanding of PO by elucidating the diagnostic complexities and clinical ramifications linked to uncommon forms of dedifferentiation, such as the simultaneous presence of leiomyosarcoma and conventional osteosarcoma. By bringing attention to this unusual presentation, we aim to stress the importance of advanced diagnostic tools and comprehensive radiologic and pathologic correlation for accurately identifying and managing such challenging cases.2

Case presentation

A 44-year-old male patient presented to the orthopedic clinic with a history of pain and swelling at the back of his left lower thigh for three months, which progressively increased over the course of the disease. Physical examination showed a minimally tender posteromedial mass with full extension and limited flexion to 100 degrees.Initial imaging with Plain radiography revealed a large, bulky, lobulated, exophytic, heavily mineralized tumor with smooth borders attached to the posterior cortex with a broad base at the metaphyseal region of the left femur (Fig. 1). Superiorly, a thin radiolucent line separates the tumor from the cortex (string sign) is noted. Multiple radiolucent foci were also observed. These imaging findings suggested parosteal osteosarcoma; however, a comprehensive differential diagnosis was considered, including high-grade surface osteosarcoma, conventional osteosarcoma with periosteal spread, and mineralized soft tissue lesions like myositis ossificans.A CT scan was ordered to confirm the nature of the mineralization and to provide more detailed information about the tumor's structure, revealing a well-circumscribed, mineralized mass with hyperdense ossified projections (Fig. 2). To evaluate the soft tissue involvement and the relationship between the tumor and surrounding structures, an MRI without contrast was also performed. It showed a well-defined tumor measuring approximately 9 × 6.5 × 4 cm at the posterior aspect of the left femur metaphysis. The tumor was inseparable from the thickened femur cortex at the site of attachment, without a periosteal reaction. The tumor had a heterogeneous signal, and its main component was ossified, displaying low signal intensity on all T1 and T2 pulse sequences (Fig. 3). The tumor displaced the adjacent muscle and neurovascular bundle with no signs of encasement or invasion. Popliteal vessels preserve a normal signal void appearance. The adjacent medullary cavity showed focal low T1 and high T2 signals, suggestive of infiltration (Fig. 5a). The tumor did not extend into the knee joint. Notably, the lack of periosteal reaction and cortical destruction helped differentiate this lesion from high-grade surface osteosarcoma and conventional osteosarcoma with periosteal spread, both of which often present with aggressive cortical disruption and prominent periosteal reactions. Additionally, certain radiological features helped rule out myositis ossificans. Unlike the well-defined and densely ossified mass observed here, myositis ossificans typically shows a “zonal phenomenon” where mineralization starts at the periphery and matures toward the center. It also tends to have a more irregular, fluffy pattern of calcification. The presence of a radiolucent line separating the mass from the underlying bone cortex was more indicative of parosteal osteosarcoma. Moreover, myositis ossificans is usually associated with a more pronounced periosteal reaction and less distinct borders, features that were not seen in this case.Due to these imaging characteristics, an open biopsy was performed, targeting the non-ossified area of the tumor. An open biopsy was performed mainly by hitting the radiolucent, non-ossified, soft tissue area from the tumor. Histologically, the biopsy showed low-grade spindle cell proliferation arranged in intersecting fascicles and exhibiting abundant brightly fibrillary eosinophilic cytoplasm, blunt-ended (cigar-shaped) nuclei, occasional perinuclear vacuoles, and frequent mitosis up to 5/10 high-power fields (HPF) (Fig. 4A and B). Osteoid or bony trabeculae were not observed. The immunohistochemical profile of the neoplastic spindle cells demonstrated diffuse strong positivity for smooth muscle actin (SMA), desmin, and H-caldesmon, consistent with smooth muscle differentiation (Fig. 4C–E). FISH testing form MDM2 gene amplification was not performed at that time, and the differential diagnosis included low-grade leiomyosarcoma with extensive mineralization and underlying bone penetration versus parosteal osteosarcoma. A few weeks later, the patient underwent a limb-sparing procedure that included wide surgical intra-articular resection of the distal femur with megaprosthesis reconstruction of the distal femur. Intraoperatively, the joint was clear from tumor involvement. The distal femur measuring 11 cm was resected along with a 1 cm proximal tibia, and the defect was reconstructed using a distal femur global modular replacement system (GMRS) from Stryker, which was a cemented implant (Fig. 5).Grossly, the specimen showed a large, well-circumscribed, lobulated hard bony tumor measuring 8 × 8 × 5.5 cm with a white, tan homogenous cut surface attached broadly to the cortical surface at the metaphyseal region encircling mainly the posteromedial aspect of the left distal femur extending focally to the medial epicondyle (Fig. 6B). Radiographically, the hard bony area of the tumor corresponded to heavily mineralized and ossified areas (Fig. 6A). A focal area of bone marrow invasion was present. There was a soft tissue nodule within the tumor with a whorly and trabeculated cut surface corresponding to the lucent area on MRI (Fig. 6A and B). The histological features of the hard bony area of the tumor showed well-formed lamellar and woven bony trabeculae arranged in interconnecting and parallel patterns, intervened by moderately cellular neoplastic spindle cell proliferation with mild to moderate atypia and mitotic activity up to 8/10 high-power fields (HPF) (Fig. 7A and B). Foci of hypercellular disorganized hyaline cartilage were noted mainly at the periphery of the mass, resembling the cartilaginous cap of the osteochondroma (Fig. 7C). Invasion of the medullary bone cavity was observed, characterized by regions of malignant lace-like osteoid containing highly atypical cells consistent with high-grade conventional osteosarcoma (Fig. 7D and D). In contrast, the soft tissue region of the tumor showed histological and immunophenotypical features of low-grade leiomyosarcomatous dedifferentiation (Fig. 7F). The tumor extended into the surrounding soft tissue; however, surgical resection margins were clear. Fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) was performed to test for MDM2 gene amplification utilizing a 4 μm thick section of formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded tissue. The MDM2 probe (Zytolight) was applied, and signals for MDM2 (green) and CEP12 (red) were counted in 30 tumor cell nuclei. The results showed an MDM2/CEP12 ratio of 8.73, indicating the presence of MDM2 gene amplification (Fig. 8). This finding confirmed the diagnosis of dedifferentiated parosteal osteosarcoma, featuring both low-grade leiomyosarcoma and high-grade conventional osteosarcoma components. The patient recovered well from surgery and was discharged from the hospital five days later, with no complications. He did not receive chemotherapy because the main tumor components were low-grade, and the surgical resection archived in clear margins, reducing the chance of residual disease. Furthermore, chemotherapy is less effective in treating low-grade components of dedifferentiated parosteal osteosarcoma, and it is usually reserved for high-grade, unresectable, or metastatic patients. The patient was closely examined with regular follow-up for six months, and there were no evidence of local recurrence or metastasis. The work has been reported in line with the SCARE criteria.3

Discussion

Dedifferentiation in parosteal osteosarcoma (PO) is a rare but well-documented phenomenon where a low-grade, well-differentiated tumor evolves into a highly malignant, poorly differentiated form. While Wold et al. [2] initially noted that dedifferentiation often occurs in recurrent PO cases, recent research from the Rizzoli Orthopedic Institute in Italy found that primary (de novo) dedifferentiation is actually more common, occurring in 32 out of 48 cases of dedifferentiated PO (DPO) [9]. This observation aligns with previous findings by Okada et al. [7], who also reported a significant number of de novo cases. The present case adds to this understanding, as dedifferentiation occurred as a primary presentation rather than following a recurrence.DPO is known to have diagnostic difficulty, as there are no specific clinical and radiological features to point toward dedifferentiation preoperatively, especially in a situation with limited biopsy samples from the dedifferentiated area, as in the present case. PO presents radiographically as a densely mineralized, well-demarcated, lobulated, mushroom-like tumor encircling the bone surface with a radiolucent line separating the base of the tumor from the adjacent cortex, which may be normal, thickened, or destroyed. As tumor grows mainly on the bone surface, there is no periosteal elevation or new bone formation. MRI aids in delineating the extent of bone and soft tissue involvement as well as highlighting cartilaginous areas. In this case, the imaging findings were consistent with classic PO characteristics, including the absence of periosteal reaction or cortical elevation, which helped distinguish it from more aggressive bone tumors like high-grade surface osteosarcoma and conventional osteosarcoma with periosteal spread. These conditions often present with pronounced cortical disruption and significant periosteal elevation, features that were notably absent here. Few studies have described the presence of radiolucent areas among heavily mineralized masses of PO radiographically, on CT, and MRI as hyperintense fluid-sensitive foci or the presence of hypervascularity and soft tissue mass correlated with dedifferentiation [6,20,21]. However, Okada et al. [7] and Lin et al. [22] reported that these findings are not definite, and it might be observed in conventional low-grade PO. In addition, it has been described in old and recent studies that medullary involvement occurs in approximately 7–65 % of PO and is commonly present with DPO; however, there is no significant difference in the prognosis and the course of the disease [9]. Lin et al. [22] studied the radiological features of 23 cases of PO (16 conventional and 7 dedifferentiated) and found that tumors larger than 11 cm and sunburst periosteal reactions are useful in discerning DPO from conventional low-grade PO, with an average sensitivity of 43 % and 57 %, respectively, and a specificity both at 87 %.The challenge in this case, although radiologically, showed features typical of PO is that the biopsy was misleading, revealing only a low-grade smooth muscle neoplasm with no osteoid or bony trabecula. Therefore, the possibility of extensively mineralized leiomyosarcoma (LMS) involving the bone was considered. LMS of the bone is very rare and commonly affects older adults in their 5th decades [23]. It has been reported that it is rarely associated with prior radiotherapy, bone infarction, and EBV infection [23]. Radiologically, it usually presents as an intramedullary, aggressive, lytic mass with cortical destruction and soft tissue involvement. Histological examination showed the typical appearance of smooth muscle neoplasm, and SMA, desmin, and H-caldesmon immunohistochemical staining will confirm the diagnosis. Rarely, LMS may exhibit extensive mineralization, creating more confusion with other entities [24], especially in limited biopsies and that the challenge we face in this case. The final diagnosis of dedifferentiated PO in the present case was confirmed only after examining the resected specimen, which showed a mix of conventional PO histology along with both low-grade leiomyosarcomatous and high-grade conventional osteosarcoma components. The uniqueness of this case lies in its combination of homologous (high-grade conventional osteosarcoma) and heterologous (low-grade leiomyosarcoma) dedifferentiation, a pattern that, to our knowledge, has not been previously reported. Most cases of dedifferentiation in PO involve transformation to high-grade conventional osteosarcoma or, less frequently, rhabdomyosarcoma [15]. The presence of a leiomyosarcomatous component in this case broadens the spectrum of known dedifferentiation patterns in PO.More than 85 % of POS showed MDM2 gene amplification on the supernumerary ring chromosome 12q13-q15, a feature rarely seen in other bone tumors except for low-grade central osteosarcoma [1]. Fortunately, this amplification also occurs in the DPO, even in the dedifferentiated area, and what we observed in our case as the leiomyosarcomatous component shows MDM2 amplification together with conventional PO. Testing for MDM2 gene amplification using FISH or other molecular methods is particularly valuable when biopsy samples are limited, helping to confirm the diagnosis and differentiate PO from other mimicking conditions. PO lacks a complex karyotype and chromosomal rearrangements, as observed in conventional high-grade osteosarcoma. Chen et al. [25] analyzed MDM2 amplification in 40 PO specimens from 14 patients and found that the level of MDM2 amplification correlated positively with the histologic grade of PO and dedifferentiation. The amplification level was higher in DPO. This finding is beneficial for identifying DPO and predicting the clinical outcome of the tumor even in small biopsy specimens.Conventional low-grade parosteal osteosarcoma has indolent behavior with excellent outcomes, and wide excision is the mainstay of treatment. In contrast, Ruengwanichayakun et al. [9] studied the clinicopathologic characteristics of 195 cases of parosteal osteosarcoma, 48 (24.6 %) were dedifferentiated and demonstrated worse 5-year (65 % versus 96 %) and 10-year survival (60 % versus 96 %) than conventional PO, confirming that the presence of dedifferentiation is the most important factor that affect the clinical course of PO adversely. As it is associated with a high risk of metastasis, and the treatment may include chemotherapy, the role of neoadjuvant chemotherapy remains controversial. Therefore, identification of the dedifferentiated area is crucial for outlining the future treatment management of the patient before starting the surgical plane.This case report describes a rare occurrence of dedifferentiated parosteal osteosarcoma (DPO) with both low-grade leiomyosarcomatous and high-grade osteosarcoma components, highlighting significant diagnostic challenges. It emphasizes the need for a multidisciplinary approach that includes imaging, histopathology, and molecular testing like MDM2 gene amplification, especially when biopsy samples are limited. Early recognition of dedifferentiation is crucial, given its impact on metastasis risk and treatment decisions. The report's limitations include a brief six-month follow-up, which is insufficient for assessing long-term risks, and the rarity of this presentation, making it difficult to generalize findings. Despite these, the case provides valuable insights into managing unusual DPO presentations and stresses the importance of personalized treatment. Surgical pathologists should be alert to rare histological patterns, as recognizing these can guide better treatment planning. This case underscores the need for tailored strategies and diligent long-term monitoring in managing rare bone tumors like DPO.

Author contribution

R.A. outlined and wrote the manuscript.R. T. collected the references and wrote the introduction.B. H. collected the references and wrote the discussion.H. A. Interpreted the radiological images.K. K. Interpreted the radiological images.B. R. contributed in writing the case presentation.

Consent

The written consent to publish the case report was obtained. This report does not contain any personal information that could lead to the identification of the patient.

Ethics statement

This case report does not require ethical approval, as it does not involve research concerning human subjects, in accordance with the guidelines established by the ethics research committee at King Abdulaziz University. The focus is on a specific clinical case, and all personal information has been managed appropriately to maintain confidentiality, ensuring that no identifiable information will be disclosed.

Guarantor

Rana Ajabnoor.

Research registration number

NA.

Funding

This study did not receive any funding.

Conflict of interest statement

The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.The case report was written following the SCARE Guidelines [26].

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 44-year-old male.
- **Chief Complaint:** Pain and swelling at the back of the left lower thigh for three months.
- **History of Present Illness:** Progressive worsening of pain and swelling over three months.
- **Past Medical History:** Not documented.
- **Personal History:** Not documented.
- **Family History:** Not documented.
- **Physical Examination:** A minimally tender posteromedial mass with restricted range of motion, full extension but flexion limited to 100 degrees.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - **Imaging:**
    - Plain radiography and CT scan: Revealed a large, mineralized tumor attached to the metaphysis of the left femur with smooth borders and a radiolucent line ("string sign") indicating parosteal osteosarcoma; absence of periosteal reaction and cortical destruction distinguished it from other surface osteosarcomas and aggressive bone tumors.
    - MRI: Showed a 9 × 6.5 × 4 cm heterogeneous ossified mass attached to the femoral cortex without periosteal reaction, with medullary infiltration but no joint involvement.
  - **Histology from biopsy:** Low-grade spindle cell proliferation consistent with smooth muscle differentiation; positive for SMA, desmin, and H-caldesmon; absence of osteoid/bony trabeculae suggested low-grade leiomyosarcoma versus parosteal osteosarcoma.
  - **Post-surgical Pathology:** Mixed features of conventional parosteal osteosarcoma alongside high-grade conventional osteosarcoma and low-grade leiomyosarcoma.
  - **FISH testing:** MDM2 gene amplification detected, confirming parosteal osteosarcoma with dedifferentiation.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Consideration of Parosteal Osteosarcoma:**
   - Indicated by imaging findings of a lobulated mineralized tumor with smooth borders and a radiolucent separation ("string sign") without aggressive cortical destruction or periosteal reaction.
   - Differential diagnoses like high-grade surface osteosarcoma and conventional osteosarcoma with periosteal spread were initially considered but ruled out due to the absence of aggressive features.

2. **Consideration of Myositis Ossificans:**
   - Ruled out because the imaging findings did not show the typical "zonal phenomenon" of peripheral mineralization or a fluffy calcification pattern, and the borders did not match the irregular presentation.

3. **Low-Grade Leiomyosarcoma Possibility:**
   - Initial biopsy only showed spindle cell proliferation consistent with leiomyosarcoma, raising ambiguity. However, this diagnosis was questioned due to the tumoral mineralization and context.

4. **Confirmation of Dedifferentiated Parosteal Osteosarcoma:**
   - Final diagnosis confirmed by examining the surgical resection specimen, which showed components of both low-grade leiomyosarcoma and high-grade conventional osteosarcoma.
   - MDM2 gene amplification provided molecular evidence distinguishing parosteal osteosarcoma from mimics like leiomyosarcoma.

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Dedifferentiated parosteal osteosarcoma (DPO) of the left femur, incorporating both low-grade leiomyosarcoma and high-grade conventional osteosarcoma components. Diagnosis confirmed through comprehensive imaging, histopathology, and molecular testing (MDM2 amplification).

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by looking at the patient's information. He's a 44-year-old male with pain and swelling at the back of his left lower thigh for three months, which has been getting worse. The physical exam shows a minimally tender mass on the posteromedial side with limited flexion to 100 degrees. 

First, the imaging results. The plain radiography and CT scan show a large mineralized tumor attached to the femur's metaphysis with smooth borders and a radiolucent line, which is the "string sign." That's a classic sign of parosteal osteosarcoma. Also, no periosteal reaction or cortical destruction, which helps rule out more aggressive tumors like conventional osteosarcoma or other surface osteosarcomas.

Next, the MRI confirms a 9 cm ossified mass attached to the femoral cortex without periosteal reaction. There's medullary infiltration but no joint involvement. Medullary invasion can sometimes complicate things, but the absence of joint issues might support a slower-growing tumor like parosteal osteosarcoma.

Then the biopsy histology shows low-grade spindle cells with smooth muscle markers (SMA, desmin, H-caldesmon), but no osteoid. This points towards leiomyosarcoma. However, parosteal osteosarcoma can sometimes mimic other tumors, especially if there's dedifferentiation. The FISH test showing MDM2 amplification is key here because MDM2 is typically amplified in parosteal osteosarcoma but not in leiomyosarcoma.

Post-surgical pathology adds more complexity. There's a mix of conventional parosteal osteosarcoma, high-grade conventional osteosarcoma, and low-grade leiomyosarcoma. Wait, that's confusing. How can there be both osteosarcoma and leiomyosarcoma? Maybe dedifferentiation in parosteal osteosarcoma can lead to high-grade areas. The MDM2 amplification supports the osteosarcoma diagnosis, even if there are leiomyosarcoma-like areas. Perhaps the leiomyosarcoma component is part of the dedifferentiated portion?

Putting it all together: The initial imaging and FISH results confirm parosteal osteosarcoma. The presence of high-grade conventional osteosarcoma in the post-surgical specimen suggests dedifferentiation, which is a known phenomenon in parosteal osteosarcoma. The low-grade leiomyosarcoma part might be a misleading histological appearance, but MDM2 amplification is more specific for osteosarcoma. Therefore, the final diagnosis is parosteal osteosarcoma with dedifferentiation into high-grade conventional osteosarcoma. The leiomyosarcoma component is likely part of the dedifferentiated areas, but the primary remains the parosteal osteosarcoma.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with a three-month history of progressive pain and swelling in the left lower thigh. Imaging (radiographs, CT, MRI) identifies a mineralized tumor attached to the femoral metaphysis with smooth borders, medullary infiltration, and a radiolucent "string sign"—features classic for parosteal osteosarcoma. The absence of periosteal reaction or cortical destruction further supports this diagnosis over aggressive surface osteosarcomas or conventional osteosarcoma.

<step 2> Histology from the biopsy reveals low-grade spindle cells with smooth muscle differentiation markers (SMA, desmin, H-caldesmon) and no osteoid. This initially raises suspicion for leiomyosarcoma. However, parosteal osteosarcoma can mimic other low-grade spindle cell tumors histologically, necessitating molecular confirmation.

<step 3> Post-surgical pathology identifies mixed features: conventional parosteal osteosarcoma, high-grade conventional osteosarcoma, and low-grade leiomyosarcoma. The presence of high-grade components suggests dedifferentiation, a recognized progression in parosteal osteosarcoma. The detection of MDM2 amplification via FISH is critical, as this genetic marker is specific to parosteal osteosarcoma and absent in leiomyosarcoma.

<step 4> The histological "leiomyosarcoma-like" component likely represents dedifferentiated areas within the parosteal osteosarcoma. MDM2 amplification confirms the primary diagnosis of parosteal osteosarcoma, overriding the morphological overlap with leiomyosarcoma. The coexistence of high-grade conventional osteosarcoma confirms dedifferentiation, a key prognostic feature.

### Answer:
Parosteal osteosarcoma with dedifferentiation (conventional high-grade osteosarcoma component).

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with a three-month history of progressive pain and swelling in the left lower thigh. Imaging (radiographs, CT, MRI) identifies a mineralized tumor attached to the femoral metaphysis with smooth borders, medullary infiltration, and a radiolucent "string sign"—features classic for parosteal osteosarcoma. The absence of periosteal reaction or cortical destruction further supports this diagnosis over aggressive surface osteosarcomas or conventional osteosarcoma.

Histology from the biopsy reveals low-grade spindle cells with smooth muscle differentiation markers (SMA, desmin, H-caldesmon) and no osteoid. This initially raises suspicion for leiomyosarcoma. However, parosteal osteosarcoma can mimic other low-grade spindle cell tumors histologically, necessitating molecular confirmation.

Post-surgical pathology identifies mixed features: conventional parosteal osteosarcoma, high-grade conventional osteosarcoma, and low-grade leiomyosarcoma. The presence of high-grade components suggests dedifferentiation, a recognized progression in parosteal osteosarcoma. The detection of MDM2 amplification via FISH is critical, as this genetic marker is specific to parosteal osteosarcoma and absent in leiomyosarcoma.

The histological "leiomyosarcoma-like" component likely represents dedifferentiated areas within the parosteal osteosarcoma. MDM2 amplification confirms the primary diagnosis of parosteal osteosarcoma, overriding the morphological overlap with leiomyosarcoma. The coexistence of high-grade conventional osteosarcoma confirms dedifferentiation, a key prognostic feature.

Step 2 Ruled out myositis ossificans because the imaging findings did not show the typical "zonal phenomenon" of peripheral mineralization or a fluffy calcification pattern, and the borders did not match the irregular presentation.
Step 3 Initial biopsy only showed spindle cell proliferation consistent with leiomyosarcoma, raising ambiguity, but this diagnosis was questioned due to the tumoral mineralization and context.

Step 6 Dedifferentiated parosteal osteosarcoma (DPO) of the left femur, incorporating both low-grade leiomyosarcoma and high-grade conventional osteosarcoma components, confirmed through comprehensive imaging, histopathology, and molecular testing (MDM2 amplification).
